characters.blurb: Persian War commander who married Agariste; Pericles inherited both Alcmaeonid blood and martial expectation.
Niece of Cleisthenes; her dream of a lion foreshadowed Pericles’s birth in the family stories.
Foreign partner blamed and praised for Pericles’s rhetoric and the Samian war; Socrates and the comics both haunt her door.
Clazomenian who taught Pericles natural philosophy; later prosecuted for impiety as a way of wounding Pericles.
Directed the great Athena and the works on the Acropolis; enemies accused him of embezzlement to strike at Pericles.
Son of Miltiades, generous and pro-Spartan. Pericles’s party ostracized him, then brought him back when Sparta snubbed him.
Twitted Pericles after the Samian triumph; Plutarch keeps her as a voice of the old Cimonian house.
Democratic reformer who broke the Areopagus’s power; assassinated, leaving Pericles heir to the popular leadership.
Organized the “good and fair” against Pericles until ostracism removed him—not the historian, though Plutarch cites that Thucydides too.

characters.relation: Spartan king in the first invasions
characters.blurb: Led Peloponnesians into Attica; Pericles’s strategy was to refuse land battle and trust the walls and fleet.
Pushed an over-bold Boeotian campaign; his disaster vindicated Pericles’s caution—too late for the dead.
Tanner-politician who inherited the people’s ear when plague and war had worn Pericles down.
Trained Pericles in music and, enemies said, in the arts of managing the demos; ostracized as too clever.

characters.relation: Son who died in the plague
characters.blurb: Legitimate son lost to the plague; Pericles’s public composure broke when he placed the burial wreath. His elder brother Xanthippus died in the same dark years.
Father of Callias; tied to the web of marriages around Pericles’s later household.
Heir of Hipponicus; appears in the gossipy edges of Aspasia’s and Alcibiades’s circle.
Young kinsman Pericles brought up—brilliant, unstable, and destined for his own Life of glory and ruin.
Old comedian who ridiculed Pericles’s “onion-head” and Aspasia; Plutarch mines the stage for contemporary color.
Eleatic thinker who commanded Samos against Athens; briefly bested Pericles at sea in the Samian war.
Hero of Oenophyta’s generation; cited among the captains who made Athens bold before Pericles’s long ascendancy.
Prophet involved in the Italian colony project—an outlet for Athens’s energy in Pericles’s peacetime.
Athenian envoy whose death sharpened the Megarian decree quarrel on the eve of the Peloponnesian War.
Dispatched with few ships—enemies said Pericles meant the name to insult Sparta while giving scant help.
Philosopher said to have taught in Pericles’s circle beside Anaxagoras.
Old guardian council stripped of political power in the reforms that opened Pericles’s democratic primacy.

characters.relation: Roman pair in the Parallel Lives
characters.blurb: Fabius Maximus, the delayer, is Pericles’s match—each a cautious savior accused of cowardice by hotter men.

Son of Xanthippus and Agariste who shaped Athens for three decades—building the Acropolis, leading the demos with gravity and restraint, and steering a cautious strategy into the Peloponnesian War. The plague took his sons and then him, and the city he had made never quite recovered.
Cited when Plutarch describes Pericles's natural endowment and the effect of Anaxagoras's philosophy on his temper—the combination that made him serene in public and cool under insult.
In the Phaedrus Plato named Pericles the greatest of all orators—trained by Anaxagoras's cosmic philosophy to speak with weight and comprehension above common politicians.
Said to have visited Aspasia and brought friends along; the anecdote illustrates how her house became a meeting-point for the finest minds of Pericles's Athens.
Thasian writer whose malicious anecdotes about Pericles, Cimon, and Themistocles Plutarch reads critically—useful for color, suspect for accuracy.

characters.relation: Tragedian who served as general with him
characters.blurb: Served as fellow general on the Samian expedition; Pericles teased him for admiring a beautiful boy, reminding him that a general must have clean hands as well as clean eyes.
Ion of Chios dined with Pericles and found him cold, haughty, and contemptuous of small talk—a contrast with Cimon's easy charm that Plutarch preserves as character testimony.
Cited for the story that Pericles prepared his speeches carefully and prayed before mounting the bema—evidence for the gravity Anaxagoras's teaching gave his public manner.

characters.relation: Engineer of the Samian siege machines
characters.blurb: Mechanic said to have designed the engines that broke down Samos's walls; his litter-bound life on site gave him the nickname Periphoretus—though Plutarch doubts the chronology.
Wrote Demes and other comedies ridiculing Pericles and his associates; Plutarch mines the stage for the hostile testimony that shadows official accounts.
Listed Pericles's allies and targets in hostile verse that Plutarch quotes as evidence for how the demos perceived his power at its height.

characters.relation: Spartan king said to have been bribed
characters.blurb: Led the Peloponnesian invasion into Attica in 446 and then withdrew without fighting; enemies of Pericles claimed he had been bribed, and the king was fined and exiled.
Commanded with Plistoanax on the Attic invasion; condemned in absentia for taking bribes, he fled to Thurii and there served as general—a cautionary tale in Pericles's Life.
Epicurean writer cited for the claim that Pericles, not Aristodicus, killed Ephialtes—a charge Plutarch finds incredible but records as part of the hostile tradition.
Athena's great chryselephantine statue by Phidias was the crown of the Parthenon and the center of the Acropolis building program Pericles directed—divine patroness and political statement in one.
Agariste's uncle who gave Athens the deme-based democracy Pericles later inherited and deepened; his blood in Pericles's veins carried both the Alcmaeonid curse and the democratic tradition.

characters.relation: Goddess whose statue rivaled Phidias's Athena
characters.blurb: Plutarch mentions the statue of Juno at Argos by Polycletus as a work that impresses but does not inspire emulation—a foil to Athena's statue that crowned Pericles's building program.

characters.relation: God invoked in comic mockery of Pericles
characters.blurb: Comedy compared Pericles to Zeus Olympian for his long head and regal bearing; the joke also appears in political context when comics styled his authority as godlike.

characters.relation: Lyric poet whose verses disprove Artemon's story
characters.blurb: His poems mention Artemon Periphoretus several generations before the Samian war—evidence Heraclides Ponticus uses to refute the tale that Artemon was the siege engineer.

characters.relation: Tyrant whose voice and manner Pericles recalled
characters.blurb: Old men who heard both noted the similarity of voice and gentleness of manner—a comparison Plutarch records without embarrassment, since Pisistratus too had once been the friend of the better sort.
Named alongside Ictinus as builder of the Parthenon—one of the master craftsmen of the Acropolis program Pericles directed.

characters.relation: Sophist; debated with Pericles on causation
characters.blurb: Leading sophist with whom Pericles spent a whole day disputing who bore moral responsibility for an athlete's accidental javelin-kill—a story his son Xanthippus mocked in public.
Pericles's firstborn, naturally prodigal, who borrowed money in his father's name, publicly reviled him when sued, and died in the plague before any reconciliation.

characters.relation: Illegitimate son by Aspasia; executed after Arginusae
characters.blurb: Born of Aspasia and legitimized by a special vote after Pericles's lawful sons died; later commanded at Arginusae, then was put to death with his fellow-generals by the Athenian assembly.
Milesian from whom Aspasia descended; the acknowledgment of her parentage is Plutarch's one certain biographical detail in the fog of comic and hostile accounts.

characters.relation: Sheep-dealer who rose by keeping Aspasia
characters.blurb: A man of low birth who, after Pericles's death, lived with Aspasia and, guided by her, became a leading figure in Athenian politics—proof of her influence.

Beautiful and sagacious Milesian woman of an earlier age whose method of attaching powerful men to the Persian interest Aspasia allegedly imitated—a hostile comparison Plutarch records without endorsing.

characters.relation: Persian satrap who helped the Samians revolt
characters.blurb: Persian king's lieutenant who offered Pericles gold to spare the Samian hostages; when rebuffed, secretly returned the hostages to Samos and equipped the city for revolt.

characters.relation: Athenian general who expelled Timesileus from Sinope
characters.blurb: Athenian commander to whom Pericles entrusted thirteen warships and troops for the Euxine expedition; he drove out Timesileus the tyrant of Sinope, allowing six hundred Athenian colonists to settle there.

characters.relation: Tyrant of Sinope expelled by Lamachus
characters.blurb: Sinopean tyrant overthrown when Pericles sent Lamachus with thirteen ships; the houses and land Timesileus's faction had held were distributed among six hundred new Athenian colonists.

characters.relation: Philosopher who refuted the Artemon story
characters.blurb: Pontic philosopher who showed from Anacreon's poems that Artemon Periphoretus lived several generations before the Samian war—dismantling Ephorus's claim that he built Pericles's siege engines.

characters.relation: Samian historian who charged Pericles with cruelty
characters.blurb: Described Pericles binding Samian prisoners to boards and clubbing them to death—an account Thucydides, Ephorus, and Aristotle ignore, and which Plutarch suspects of patriotic exaggeration.

characters.relation: Historian who reported the Samian siege engines
characters.blurb: Cited for the claim that Pericles used siege engines at Samos with Artemon's help; also named alongside Thucydides and Aristotle as a source that contradicts Duris's atrocity story.
Athenian comedian of the fifth century who introduces Chiron questioning Damon about teaching Pericles—one of many comic sources Plutarch mines for evidence of public perceptions.
Aristotle attributes Pericles's musical education to Pythoclides rather than to Damon—one of the minor disputes in the ancient tradition about who formed the young statesman.
Founder of the Eleatic school; Zeno's treatment of natural philosophy followed his approach, and both men were said to have influenced the circle around Pericles.
Sceptic poet-philosopher whose line about Zeno's two-edged tongue that could argue any proposition false Plutarch quotes to characterize the eristic tradition Pericles absorbed.
Master sculptor whose Juno at Argos Plutarch cites as a masterwork that impresses without inspiring emulation—a foil for Phidias's Athena and the standard of comparison for physical beauty in art.

characters.relation: Peripatetic philosopher who described Pericles's reserve
characters.blurb: Peripatetic thinker cited for the observation that Pericles held himself back from public life like the Salaminia, reserved for great occasions—an image of deliberate political economy.
Aeschines of Sphettus, Socratic philosopher and writer, reported that Lysicles the sheep-dealer rose to prominence through his association with Aspasia after Pericles died.

characters.relation: Persian prince who named a concubine Aspasia
characters.blurb: Cyrus the Younger, who fought Artaxerxes for the throne, loved a Phocaean concubine named Milto so much he renamed her Aspasia after Athens's most famous woman.
Worked alongside Callicrates to build the Parthenon, the crown of Pericles's Acropolis program—one of the craftsmen through whom, Pericles told the assembly, all Attica was put on state-pay.
Principal architect of the great gateway to the Acropolis, completed in five years; a remarkable feat during which, Plutarch records, a miraculous cure showed Athena's approval.

characters.relation: Builder who began the Eleusis chapel
characters.blurb: Started the chapel at Eleusis where the Mysteries were held, raising the colonnade from the pavement up to the architraves before his death left the work unfinished.

characters.relation: Architect who completed the Eleusis chapel
characters.blurb: Of Xypete; added the frieze and upper colonnade to the chapel at Eleusis after Coroebus died, continuing the Periclean building program at the most sacred site in Attica.

characters.relation: Architect who roofed the temple of Castor and Pollux
characters.blurb: Of Cholargus; arched the lantern on the temple of the Dioscuri—one of the named craftsmen who carried out the wider program of building Pericles directed.

characters.relation: Comedian who indicted Aspasia; mocked Pericles
characters.blurb: Athenian comic playwright who formally charged Aspasia with impiety and procuring women for Pericles, and wrote anapaestic verses mocking the general's caution in the war.

characters.relation: Proposer of the harsh Megarian decree
characters.blurb: After the herald Anthemocritus was killed, Charinus proposed the decree of irreconcilable enmity against Megara—twice yearly invasion, and death to any Megarian who entered Attica.
Moved that persons who neglected religion or taught new doctrines about the heavens should be publicly prosecuted—the decree whose real target was Pericles, with Anaxagoras as the opening case.
A former workman of Phidias whom enemies of Pericles tempted into the market-place as a public informer, to test how Athenian juries would treat Pericles's circle.
Removed the most onerous clause from Dracontides's motion—that Pericles account for expenditure before the Prytanes—and substituted a jury trial of fifteen hundred.

characters.relation: Moved decree that Pericles account for public money
characters.blurb: Proposed that Pericles deposit his accounts with the Prytanes to be judged at the altar on the Acropolis—the political attack that directly threatened him at the height of the war.

characters.relation: Samian tyrant who invented the samaena warship
characters.blurb: Tyrant of Samos said to have ordered the first broad-beamed, snub-nosed galley—the samaena—that the Athenians branded on their Samian prisoners in the Periclean war.

characters.relation: Adviser who suggested public money for the poor
characters.blurb: Of Oea; Aristotle credits him with the key advice to Pericles—that distributing state funds for public duties and shows was the way to outbid Cimon's private generosity.
Hired by the oligarchical faction to murder Ephialtes, Pericles's reforming ally; Aristotle names him as the killer—correcting the hostile tradition that blamed Pericles himself.

characters.relation: Democratic statesman of the age before Pericles
characters.blurb: Named alongside Ephialtes, Myronides, Tolmides, and Cimon as the generation of leaders against whose authority Pericles held the first place for forty years.

characters.relation: Son of Cleandrides; ruiner of Athens in Sicily
characters.blurb: Son of the Spartan general Cleandrides who took Pericles's bribe; Gylippus inherited his father's dishonesty and used his military gifts to destroy the Athenian expedition in Sicily.

characters.relation: Friend of Pericles slandered in comedy
characters.blurb: A friend who served as lieutenant under Pericles; comic poets falsely alleged Pericles had an affair with his wife—a sample of the ribaldry Plutarch deplores but records.
Friend of Pericles who kept exotic peacocks; comics claimed he gave them as presents to women who visited Pericles—another strand of the hostile tradition about Aspasia's household.

characters.relation: Spartan ambassador who suggested turning the tablet
characters.blurb: Made the wry remark—"turn it around, there's no law against that"—when Pericles refused to take down the Megarian decree tablet; his wit did not move Pericles one step.

characters.relation: Father of the Persian concubine renamed Aspasia
characters.blurb: Phocaean father of Milto, the concubine Cyrus the Younger loved so much he renamed her Aspasia—a Persian echo of Pericles's companion that Plutarch inserts as a curious digression.

characters.relation: Persian concubine renamed Aspasia by Cyrus
characters.blurb: Phocaean woman of great beauty who came to Cyrus the Younger's court; renamed Aspasia after Athens's most famous woman, she outlived Cyrus and had great influence at the Persian court.
The single servant Pericles entrusted with running his entire domestic economy—selling produce in bulk, buying provisions as needed—the man who made the statesman's famously careful budget work.

The one person whose wedding feast Pericles attended, though even then he left before the drinking was finished—the single recorded exception to his rule of avoiding all social dinners, cited to show how jealously he guarded his public image.

characters.relation: Father of Thucydides the political opponent
characters.blurb: Father of the aristocratic leader Thucydides who organized conservative opposition to Pericles; mentioned to distinguish this Thucydides (son of Melesias) from the historian Thucydides son of Olorus.
Father of the rash general Tolmides who attacked Boeotia against Pericles's advice; after the disaster at Coronea that prediction was remembered as one of Pericles's wisest political acts.
Father of Melissus of Samos, the Eleatic philosopher who commanded the Samian fleet; when Pericles left with a detachment Melissus attacked, defeated the remaining squadron, and prolonged the siege.

characters.relation: Father of Xanthippus the Younger's expensive wife
characters.blurb: His daughter married Xanthippus, Pericles's eldest son; her expensive tastes led Xanthippus to borrow money in his father's name and, when sued, to publicly slander Pericles to his guests.

characters.relation: Grandfather of Xanthippus the Younger's wife
characters.blurb: Father of Tisander; mentioned only to identify Xanthippus's bride by her grandfather's name—a sign of the family's prominence and of how closely Plutarch traces the household tensions that embittered Pericles's last years.

characters.relation: Pharsalian killed by javelin at the games
characters.blurb: Struck accidentally by a javelin thrown by Pericles's son; the incident prompted Pericles to spend a full day with Protagoras debating whether the fault lay with the javelin, the thrower, or the games officials—an anecdote that anchors the abstract sophistic argument in real sorrow.

characters.relation: Third son of Cimon; born of an Arcadian woman
characters.blurb: One of three sons Cimon had by an Arcadian woman—the others being Lacedaemonius and Thessalus—who were spoken of disparagingly because Cimon supplied them only ten galleys when Athens needed far more.

characters.relation: Second son of Cimon; born of an Arcadian woman
characters.blurb: One of the three foreign-born sons of Cimon (with Lacedaemonius and Eleus), criticised for providing only ten ships to the Athenian cause; the same Thessalus later filed the formal impiety charge against Alcibiades.

nameResolutions.note: Father of Pericles, victor at Mycale
Pericles's eldest son Xanthippus, listed with his brother Paralus
The son Xanthippus, disobliged by his father
